QF.id: Role of RTAs in Mutual Fund Transactions_18

question.text: What is the key difference between open-ended and closed-ended mutual fund schemes?

answer.text: Open-ended schemes have a fixed number of units while closed-ended schemes have variable units
Open-ended schemes charge higher TER than closed-ended schemes due to more frequent transactions
Open-ended schemes allow continuous subscription and redemption at NAV, while closed-ended schemes have a fixed maturity and must be listed on stock exchanges
Open-ended schemes invest only in equities while closed-ended schemes invest only in debt

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Open-ended schemes have variable units (new units created on purchase, cancelled on redemption) and transact at NAV continuously. Closed-ended schemes issue a fixed number of units during the NFO, after which they must be compulsorily listed on exchanges for liquidity. **The Trap:** The descriptions are sometimes reversed. Remember: OPEN = open door (come and go freely), CLOSED = closed door (fixed units, exchange-listed for secondary market access). **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A covers scheme types extensively. The distinction affects how RTAs process transactions -- open-ended at NAV daily, closed-ended primarily through exchange. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For clients who need liquidity, always prefer open-ended schemes. Closed-ended schemes may trade at a discount to NAV on exchanges, meaning the client gets less than the underlying value.
